name: first-dyed-flax-fibers
description: Wild flax fibers found in a Georgian cave were twisted and colored, the earliest known dyed textile material.

name: bone-eyed-needle
description: Ice age hunters carved bone needles with eyes, allowing fitted, sewn clothing that aided survival in cold climates.

name: venus-of-willendorf-attire
description: Paleolithic Venus figurines depict woven caps and string skirts, hinting at early textile craft in ice age Europe.

name: first-woven-linen
description: Neolithic weavers in the Near East spun and wove flax into linen, producing the earliest true woven fabric.

name: wool-textile-production
description: Selective breeding of woolly sheep let early peoples spin and weave wool, a warm and renewable fiber for clothing.

name: otzi-clothing
description: The frozen Alpine mummy wore a grass cloak, leather leggings and bearskin cap, preserving Copper Age dress.

name: egyptian-kohl-eyeliner
description: Ancient Egyptians lined their eyes with kohl, a lead-based cosmetic believed to protect against sun glare and disease.

name: egyptian-linen-pleating
description: Egyptian elites wore finely pleated white linen garments, a sign of status and skilled craft along the Nile.

name: indus-cotton-cultivation
description: Indus Valley artisans cultivated and spun cotton into cloth, the first large-scale cotton textile industry.

name: tyrian-purple-dye
description: Phoenicians extracted prized purple dye from sea snails, a costly color reserved for royalty and the elite.

name: egyptian-wig-fashion
description: Wealthy Egyptians shaved their heads and wore elaborate wigs of human hair scented with perfumed cones.

name: tutankhamun-gold-sandals
description: The boy pharaoh was buried in gold sandals and jeweled regalia, showcasing New Kingdom royal adornment.

name: greek-chiton-garment
description: Ancient Greeks draped the chiton, a simple pinned tunic of linen or wool that defined classical dress.

name: roman-toga-adopted
description: The draped woolen toga became the formal garment of Roman male citizens, marking their legal status and rank.

name: celtic-tartan-weaving
description: Iron Age Celtic peoples wove brightly checked woolen cloth, an early ancestor of later Scottish tartan.

name: roman-fibula-brooch
description: Romans fastened cloaks with the fibula, a decorative pin functioning much like a modern safety pin.

name: roman-leather-shoes-caligae
description: Roman legionaries marched in caligae, hobnailed leather sandals built for durability on long campaigns.

name: roman-sumptuary-laws
description: Rome passed laws restricting luxury dress and purple cloth, limiting displays of wealth to certain ranks.

name: byzantine-silk-monopoly
description: Monks smuggled silkworm eggs to Constantinople, breaking China's monopoly and founding Byzantine silk weaving.

name: viking-wool-sails-clothing
description: Norse households spun and wove wool into tunics, cloaks and sails, a labor-intensive backbone of Viking life.

name: japanese-kimono-emerges
description: During the Heian period the straight-cut kimono took shape, becoming Japan's enduring traditional garment.

name: chinese-foot-binding-begins
description: Chinese elites began binding girls' feet to alter their shape, a painful beauty practice lasting nearly a millennium.

name: medieval-pointed-shoes
description: Fashionable medieval Europeans wore poulaines, shoes with exaggerated long points that signaled wealth and leisure.

name: houppelande-court-gown
description: Late medieval European courts favored the houppelande, a voluminous flowing robe with deep trailing sleeves.

name: burgundian-court-fashion
description: The wealthy court of Burgundy led European fashion, with towering hennin headdresses and rich brocades.

name: high-heeled-shoes-persia
description: Persian cavalry wore raised heels to grip stirrups, a style that spread to European aristocrats as status footwear.

name: corset-becomes-fashion
description: Stiffened corsets entered European fashion, reshaping women's waists and dominating dress for over three centuries.

name: elizabethan-ruff-collar
description: Starched pleated ruff collars framed the necks of Elizabethan nobility, growing ever larger as a sign of status.

name: farthingale-hoop-skirt
description: The farthingale used hoops to hold skirts in a wide bell or drum shape, defining elite women's silhouettes.

name: necktie-cravat-origin
description: Croatian mercenaries' neck scarves inspired the French cravat, the ancestor of the modern necktie.

name: powdered-wig-fashion
description: Louis XIV popularized large powdered wigs, which became essential formal dress for European men of rank.

name: stiletto-three-piece-suit
description: Charles II of England introduced the vest and coat ensemble, founding the lasting three-piece suit for men.

name: panniers-wide-skirts
description: Side hoops called panniers extended skirts dramatically outward at the hips in 18th-century European courts.

name: marie-antoinette-pouf
description: French court fashion reached extremes with towering pouf hairstyles adorned with feathers, ribbons and models.

name: umbrella-popularized
description: Jonas Hanway championed the umbrella in London, transforming it from an oddity into a common accessory.

name: top-hat-introduced
description: The tall silk top hat debuted in London, becoming the defining formal headwear of 19th-century gentlemen.

name: macintosh-raincoat
description: Charles Macintosh patented a rubberized fabric, creating the first practical waterproof raincoat.

name: crinoline-cage-skirt
description: The steel-hooped crinoline let women support enormous skirts without heavy petticoats, a Victorian craze.

name: bowler-hat-invented
description: London hatters Thomas and William Bowler designed a hard rounded hat for gamekeepers that became a city icon.

name: levis-blue-jeans-patent
description: Levi Strauss and Jacob Davis patented riveted denim work trousers, the origin of modern blue jeans.

name: rubber-sole-sneakers
description: Keds-style canvas shoes with rubber soles, dubbed sneakers for their quiet tread, brought comfort to footwear.

name: burberry-trench-coat
description: Burberry refined a weatherproof gabardine coat for British officers in World War I, later a fashion staple.

name: modern-bra-patented
description: Mary Phelps Jacob patented a lightweight backless brassiere, offering an alternative to the restrictive corset.

name: flapper-dress-1920s
description: Jazz Age women embraced loose, knee-length flapper dresses that freed the body and broke with Victorian dress.

name: chanel-little-black-dress
description: Coco Chanel introduced a simple, elegant black dress that Vogue hailed as a timeless wardrobe essential.

name: nylon-stockings-debut
description: DuPont's nylon stockings hit American stores and sold millions of pairs, revolutionizing women's hosiery.

name: bikini-introduced
description: Designer Louis Reard debuted the two-piece bikini in Paris, named after a nuclear test site for its impact.

name: diors-new-look
description: Christian Dior's New Look brought back full skirts and nipped waists, redefining postwar women's fashion.

name: stiletto-heel-invented
description: Slim, towering stiletto heels emerged in Italian and French fashion, becoming an icon of glamour.

name: velcro-patented
description: Swiss engineer George de Mestral patented the hook-and-loop fastener inspired by burrs clinging to his dog.

name: miniskirt-popularized
description: Mary Quant popularized the miniskirt in swinging London, making the daring hemline a symbol of the era.

name: lycra-spandex-invented
description: DuPont chemist Joseph Shivers invented spandex, an elastic fiber that transformed activewear and form-fitting dress.

name: air-jordan-sneakers
description: Nike released the first Air Jordan basketball shoe, igniting the global sneaker culture and collector market.

name: fast-fashion-rise
description: Retailers like Zara and H&M scaled rapid, low-cost clothing turnover, reshaping how the world buys fashion.
